title: Rule of Nines
subtitle: Estimate burn size, then connect the estimate to transport and treatment implications.
pediatric_notes: Infants and young children have proportionally larger heads and smaller legs than adults.
Use Rule of Nines as a field estimate; pediatric burn charts or local burn-center tools refine the estimate.
Burns to face, hands, feet, genitalia, major joints, circumferential areas, or with airway concern can drive transport even when BSA is smaller.

vignettes.prompt: A 10-year-old spilled hot soup across the front of the chest and abdomen. No arm, back, face, or leg involvement is noted.
vignettes.hint: Focus on one full anterior surface of the trunk.
vignettes.explanation: The anterior torso is estimated at 18% TBSA by adult Rule of Nines.

follow_up.prompt: What implication matters most from this estimate?
follow_up.correct: Large partial-thickness burn: treat pain, prevent heat loss, and consider burn-center consultation/transport.
distractors: Minor isolated burn: cool briefly and release after refusal paperwork.
Airway burn is confirmed by BSA alone.
No transport concern unless hypotension is already present.
follow_up.explanation: BSA helps determine burn severity and transport/consultation needs; it does not by itself confirm airway injury.

vignettes.prompt: A 7-year-old has partial-thickness burns to the entire left arm after a sleeve caught fire. No torso involvement.
vignettes.hint: One complete upper extremity is a single Rule of Nines region.
vignettes.explanation: One complete arm is estimated at 9% TBSA.

follow_up.prompt: Which follow-up finding would most increase concern?
follow_up.correct: Circumferential burn with swelling or worsening distal pulse/sensation.
distractors: A small intact blister away from a joint.
Pain that improves after appropriate analgesia.
Normal capillary refill in all fingers.
follow_up.explanation: Circumferential extremity burns can threaten perfusion as swelling progresses.

vignettes.prompt: A child pulled boiling water onto both legs. Burns are circumferential from thighs through feet bilaterally.
vignettes.hint: Count each complete lower extremity separately.
vignettes.explanation: Both complete legs total 36% TBSA by adult Rule of Nines.

follow_up.prompt: What is the safest field implication?
follow_up.correct: High-risk burn: prevent heat loss, monitor perfusion, manage pain, and prioritize burn-capable transport/consultation.
distractors: Delay transport until blisters are drained.
Apply ice packs broadly to stop ongoing tissue damage.
Treat as minor because no torso is burned.
follow_up.explanation: Large burns can cause fluid loss, hypothermia, severe pain, and perfusion compromise; avoid ice and avoid delaying transport.

vignettes.prompt: A house-fire patient has burns to the head/neck and anterior torso with soot around the mouth.
vignettes.hint: Add the head/neck region to the front of the trunk.
vignettes.explanation: Head/neck 9% plus anterior torso 18% equals 27% TBSA.

follow_up.prompt: Which implication takes priority beyond the percentage?
follow_up.correct: Airway/inhalation concern: high-flow oxygen, early airway vigilance, rapid transport/ALS as appropriate.
distractors: BSA is below 30%, so airway assessment can wait.
Soot confirms cyanide poisoning as the only concern.
Cover burns first; oxygen can wait until vitals worsen.
follow_up.explanation: Face/neck burns and soot are airway red flags independent of the exact BSA estimate.

vignettes.prompt: A toddler has burns limited to the perineum after sitting in hot bath water.
vignettes.hint: The special-area region is small by percentage but important by location.
vignettes.explanation: The perineum is estimated at 1% TBSA.

follow_up.prompt: Why is this still high significance?
follow_up.correct: Special-area burn and concerning mechanism/history: document carefully, manage pain, and consider abuse/neglect concerns per protocol.
distractors: Only 1% TBSA means no assessment beyond cooling is needed.
Perineal burns are never suspicious in toddlers.
BSA estimate replaces the need to assess mechanism.
follow_up.explanation: Small BSA burns can still require escalation because of location, age, and mechanism/history.

vignettes.prompt: A teenager has partial-thickness burns across the entire back after falling against a campfire grate.
vignettes.hint: Think posterior trunk, not both front and back.
vignettes.explanation: The posterior torso is estimated at 18% TBSA.

follow_up.prompt: Which care priority best matches this presentation?
follow_up.correct: Cover with dry sterile dressings, prevent heat loss, treat pain, and reassess for associated trauma.
distractors: Pack the burn with wet towels during transport.
Ignore pain until after arrival because burns are external.
Assume no trauma because the injury is thermal.
follow_up.explanation: Burn care includes dry coverage, heat-loss prevention, analgesia, and mechanism-based trauma reassessment.
